coredata.dc:title: A novel composite material with wood-based carbon quantum dots modified Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Xing
Ji, Xiaodi
Nguyen, Tat Thang
Gong, Xinchao
Chai, Rusong
Guo, Minghui
coredata.dc:description: Composite materials made from renewable resources are challenging, requiring innovative research. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained via a simple hydrothermal method using waste larch wood as the raw material without the use of toxic reagents. The CQDs have a delocalized conjugated structure and can cause rapid photogenerated electron-hole pair separation and a relatively slow recombination. They also emit blue-green light upon independent excitation and are potential candidates for use as novel activators. 3D Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres are expected to become competitive structures, since they can be synthesized via a mixed solvothermal method without the need for added surfactants or other hard-template conditions. The formation mechanism of Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres was systematically examined. Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres were selected in this research as the matrix and were doped with CQDs to synthesize a new composite material, CQDs/Bi2MoO6. It was observed that methylene blue could be degraded by Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres using visible light irradiation, and the photocatalytic performance was increased when modified with CQDs. Additionally, the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs was enhanced via the addition of CQDs to Bi2MoO6. Thus, the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system was more effective for the degradation of methylene blue when irradiated using visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres
CQDs/Bi2MoO6
Surface modification
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation mechanism of Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres
The visible light-response mechanism of CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS survey spectrum (a), XPS C 1s peaks and fitting curves (b), XPS O 1s peaks and fitting curves (c).
FT-IR spectra of the original wood powder (a) and CQDs (b).
UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs (a), CIE chromaticity diagram of CQDs upon excitation at 350 nm, 380 nm, 400 nm and 420 nm (b).
The emission and excitation spectra of CQDs (a), PL emission spectra of CQDs with different excitation wavelengths (b), fluorescence decay curve of CQDs irradiated at 350 nm (c).
SEM images of Bi2MoO6 at different formation stages of 0.5 h (a), 6 h (b) and 8 h (c).
Schematic illustration of the formation of the Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres.
TEM images of Bi2MoO6 (a), CQDs/Bi2MoO6 (b), and CQDs (diameter distribution of CQDs in internal diagram) (c).
XRD patterns of CQDs (a), Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Bi2MoO6 (b).
Raman spectra of Bi2MoO6 (a), CQDs (b), and CQDs/Bi2MoO6 (c).
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Bi2MoO6 (a), Plots of (αhν)1/2 versus photon energy (hν) (b).

Comparison of the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under visible light irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
EIS variations of Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Bi2MoO6 film electrode under visible light irradiation (a), Photoluminescence emission spectra of Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Bi2MoO6 under 278 nm excitation (b).

Effects of tert-butanol (a), H2O2 (b) and K2S2O8 (c) on CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system for the degradation of methylene blue under visible light irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The schematic diagram of the working mechanism excited by visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: A novel composite material with wood-based carbon quantum dots modified Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology (Ministry of Education), College of Material Science and Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bio-based Material Science and Technology (Ministry of Education)
College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Forestry, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi, 712100, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vietnam National University of Forestry, Hanoi, 156220, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Forest Botanical Garden, Harbin, 150040, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Composite materials made from renewable resources are challenging, requiring innovative research. In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were obtained via a simple hydrothermal method using waste larch wood as the raw material without the use of toxic reagents. The CQDs have a delocalized conjugated structure and can cause rapid photogenerated electron-hole pair separation and a relatively slow recombination. They also emit blue-green light upon independent excitation and are potential candidates for use as novel activators. 3D Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres are expected to become competitive structures, since they can be synthesized via a mixed solvothermal method without the need for added surfactants or other hard-template conditions. The formation mechanism of Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres was systematically examined. Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres were selected in this research as the matrix and were doped with CQDs to synthesize a new composite material, CQDs/Bi2MoO6. It was observed that methylene blue could be degraded by Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres using visible light irradiation, and the photocatalytic performance was increased when modified with CQDs. Additionally, the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs was enhanced via the addition of CQDs to Bi2MoO6. Thus, the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system was more effective for the degradation of methylene blue when irradiated using visible light.

ce:section.ce:para: At the beginning of the 21st century, scientists from Clemson University developed a novel carbon nanomaterial: carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [1]. CQDs, also known as carbon dots, carbon nanodots, and carbon nanocrystals, are composed of sp2 and unsaturated sp3 hybrid carbon atoms that exist as delocalized conjugated systems [2] and are monodisperse and have a nearly quasi-spherical geometry [3]. They can be divided into oil-soluble carbon dots and water-soluble carbon dots. The water-soluble carbon dots have many water-soluble groups on their surface, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups and are compatible with a variety of inorganic and organic molecules [4]. Since the discovery of carbon quantum dots, many methods have been developed to synthesize CQDs, including laser ablation [2], chemical oxidation [5], and microwave methods [6]. The hydrothermal method [7] is a type of thermal decomposition method that is simple in operation, low-cost, and environmentally-friendly compared to other preparation methods. Synthesis is relatively mild and the yield of carbon quantum dots which have good water solubility, high fluorescence properties and high crystallinity and purity is high [7,8].

Waste wood is recyclable garbage that is often ignored, resulting in a low recycling rate and serious waste of wood materials. Compared to carbohydrates, the high carbon (49–50%) and oxygen (45–50%) elements and functional surface groups allow low-value waste wood to be pyrolyzed to provide new carbon materials for use as sustainable fillers for composites. Using wood as the carbon source is an efficient way to prepare carbon quantum dots using environmentally-friendly hydrothermal conditions, which has a straightforward process flow [9], as well as a low production cost and results in less pollution. This method not only provides a new way to utilize waste wood but also provides a new raw material for the preparation of carbon quantum dots. Recently, researchers have discovered that the delocalized conjugated structure of CQDs can cause fast photogenerated electron-hole pair separation and a relatively slow recombination [2], such as in a characteristic electron-transfer process. The activity of a photocatalyst can be enhanced by improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thus further research is needed to discover a potential system for the modification of photocatalysts by CQDs.

In recent years, the development of photocatalysts sensitive to visible light has become one of the most active topics in photocatalytic research, and some novel, complex photocatalysts that are responsive to visible light have been developed [10,11]. Bi2MoO6 is an Aurivillius-type semiconductor photocatalyst with a layered structure that is composed of alternating (Bi2O2)2+ layers and co-angled MoO6 perovskite sheets that can be used for the treatment of organic pollutants in wastewater. The preparation of Bi2MoO6, using methods such as the hydrothermal method, the molten salt method, and the ultrasonic reaction method, has been reported [12,13]. However, according to the anisotropic growth characteristics of Bi2MoO6, most prepared Bi2MoO6 crystals have a structure that is composed of 2D nanosheets. The microstructure of semiconductor materials is well-documented to have a significant impact on their physicochemical properties. Numerous studies have shown that the optics, catalysis, magnetism, and electrical properties of semiconductor materials are closely related to their morphologies [14,15]. Semiconductor materials with hollow microsphere structures, low densities, and high specific surface areas can produce interesting microscopic “wrapping” effect properties that are useful to many fields, such as drug delivery vehicles, optics, and catalysis. Since they are a new type of structure, the construction of Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres is currently a challenge [16]. Some reports have demonstrated that semiconductor photocatalytic materials with hollow microsphere structures can be used to degrade organic contaminants in wastewater. However, these materials are almost all single oxides, and composite oxide semiconductor photocatalytic materials are rare. Bismuth-based composite oxides, such as Bi2MoO6, respond to visible light, but the photoelectron-hole pair recombination rate is relatively fast, which is not conducive to photocatalysis. This shortcoming greatly limits the application of these semiconductors in many photocatalytic materials and hinders their commercial development [17–19]. To improve the separation of photoelectron-hole pairs and reduce the probability of their recombination, some researchers have modified these composite oxides by doping them with rare earth ions or metal ions, such as In3+, Ga3+, and A13+ [20] to create new types of highly-efficient composite materials [17]. In this research, CQDs containing abundant functional groups doped onto Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres are shown to affect the photocatalytic properties which are worth exploring. Additionally, there is currently no report detailing CQDs/Bi2MoO6 composite photocatalytic materials, and further investigation needed.

With regards to the interesting properties of hollow sphere structures, Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres can be prepared via an ethylene glycol/anhydrous ethanol mixed solvothermal method. Based on the wood-based CQDs with delocalized conjugate structures, it is postulated that the enhanced photoactivity of the CQDs modified with the Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst is a result of the increased migration efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pair.

Waste larch wood was supplied by Heilongjiang Forest Botanical Garden (China). Larch off-cuts were ground into powder, sifted through 80–100 mesh, and dissolved in deionized water. The solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave cooled to room temperature, the resulting brown solution was collected by centrifugation at a speed of 8000 r/min for 30 min to remove any solid products. The resulting liquid was dialyzed (1000 μ) for 3 days until the liquid was colorless and was then filtered through 0.22 μm filters to remove large particles and placed in a vacuum freeze dryer. The dried solid sample was labeled as CQDs before further characterization.

All reagents, including Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, ethylene glycol, urea, anhydrous ethanol, methylene blue, H2O2, tert-butanol and K2S2O8 were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology (China) and used as received without further purification. A mixed solvothermal method with ethylene glycol/anhydrous ethanol was used to synthesize the Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres. 2.5 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 0.179 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O were dispersed in ethylene glycol and agitated ultrasonically over a period of 10 min until all the solids were dissolved. Ultrasonication was performed at 25 °C, using a water level of 40 mm, a power of 60%, and a frequency of 60 kHz. A 30 mL absolute ethanol solution with 0.3 g urea was added, stirred, and then transferred to an autoclave. The mixed solution was heated at 160 °C for 12 h, cooled to room temperature, and then the precipitates were washed repeatedly with deionized water and ethanol until a neutral solution was obtained. The precipitates were dried at 80 °C for 12 h, ground, and then stored until further use.

One gram of the Bi2MoO6 photocatalysts powder was placed into a 50 mL beaker with 10 mL of CQDs aqueous solution and stirred for 12 h. The products were transferred into a blast drying oven and dried to obtain a CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd, Tokyo, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained using an SU1510 scanning electron microscope to determine the growth mechanism of samples. The elemental composition and chemical bonding configurations of the CQDs were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a PHI5700 spectrometer (Physical Electronics, Inc, Chanhassen, MN, USA) equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed using a Nicolet 6700 Frontier system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Inc, Madison, WI, USA), using an operating frequency range of 500–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of the samples were obtained using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with an operating energy of 40 kV/100 mA. Raman spectra were recorded using a JY HR800 laser Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon SAS, Paris, France) with a 514 nm laser beam. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded over the range 200–800 nm using a TU-1901 ultraviolet–visible double-beam spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were obtained using an FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, U.K.) using a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. A PL decay curve was recorded at room temperature using the time-correlated double-photon counting technique for CQDs transitions, with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Electrochemical measurements employed a basic electro-chemical system, a working electrode (prepared film electrode), and a reference electrode (SCE). A 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution was used as electrolyte solution.

The photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue was performed under visible light irradiation using a 380 W Xe lamp with a 510 nm cut filter. In each experiment, 0.1 g of the photocatalysts were dispersed into methylene blue (MB) solution (5 mg L−1) for 5 min, and the dispersion was kept under constant air-equilibrated conditions before and during irradiation by visible light. The samples were magnetically stirred, and dark adsorption was carried out for 0.5 h with illumination so that simulated contaminants in the solution reached an adsorption-desorption equilibrium on the surface of the photocatalyst powder. The light source was turned on, and sampling was carried out every 0.5 h (extracting 5 mL each time) over a period of 2 h.

The chemical composition of the CQDs was characterized by XPS, and three peaks were seen at 284.8 eV, 531.7 eV, and 399.6 eV, which were attributed to C 1s (49.54%), O 1s (47.78%), and N 1s (2.68%) (Fig. 1 a–c). The C 1s spectrum can be divided into three curves: C-C/C=C/C-H (283.7 eV), C-O (286.3 eV), and C=O (287.9 eV) [6]. The O 1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 531.1 eV, 532.4 eV, and 533.2 eV corresponding to C=O, C-O, and C-OH, respectively. The presence of high contents of C and O confirmed that the CQDs are formed from the concentrated carbonaceous material and are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups.

The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 a and b) also helped determine the surface groups present on both the CQDs and the original wood powder. The broad peak at 3000-3500 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the O-H groups [2], while the three peaks at 1451 cm−1, 2877 cm−1, and 2884 cm−1 corresponded to the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of methyl or methylene groups [5]. The peaks at 1315 cm−1 and 1310 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C=C bonds [2], and the peaks at 1700 cm−1 and 1417 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of carboxyl groups. The peaks at 1624 cm−1 and 1646 cm−1 corresponded to aldehyde groups, and the peaks at 1512 cm−1 and 1538 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of a benzene ring [8]. The FT-IR spectra demonstrate that the structure of the original wood powder is partially destroyed during the hydrothermal process, and some groups, such as benzene rings and aldehydes are pyrolyzed, which form the CQDs whose surfaces are rich in hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups, and methyl or methylene groups. The presence of abundant oxygen-containing groups improved the hydrophilicity and stability of the CQDs in water, which laid the foundation for the subsequent preparation of composite materials.

The UV–vis and PL spectra show that the CQDs have favorable optical properties. The UV–vis spectrum shows a weak peak at 267 nm (Fig. 3 a), which is attributed to a complex electron transition on the surface associated with the typical absorptions of delocalized π-bond conjugated systems and n-p* transitions of other oxygen-containing groups [21]. The UV–vis spectra demonstrates that the CQDs have a delocalized conjugated structure, which laid the foundation for the subsequent modification of Bi2MoO6. The positions of the CIE chromaticity are shown in Fig. 3b, which shows that the aqueous solution of CQDs exhibits a bright blue-green luminescence.

The PL excitation (left) and emission (right) spectra (Fig. 4 a) show that a direct relationship exists between the maximum emission intensity and the excitation wavelength. The emission spectrum was obtained by detecting the excitation at 350 nm and obtaining a emission peak that displayed a maximum at 434 nm. PL emission spectra were obtained using different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4b). When the excitation wavelength was increased from 300 to 350 nm, the emission wavelength red-shifted from 428 to 434 nm, accompanied by an increase in intensity [22]. By changing the excitation wavelength from 360 to 440 nm (Fig. 4b), the emission peak gradually red-shifted from 439 to 515 nm [23] with a gradual decrease in intensity. A typical decay curve for the luminescence of the CQDs at 434 nm (λex = 350 nm) is shown in Fig. 4c. This curve can be fitted to a double-exponential function since the mean lifetime model for calculating the total life of time-resolved fluorescence spectra is τ = Ʃ An τn 2/An τn, (n = 1, 2, 3 …...). The time-resolved fluorescence spectrum fitting model is an E-index model where y = y0 + Ʃ An e-x/τn, (n = 1, 2, 3 …...), and the luminescence lifetime was calculated to be 0.4595 ns.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation mechanism of Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the Bi2MoO6 samples at different solvothermal reaction times were observed to investigate the formation process of Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres. The SEM images of Bi2MoO6 samples at different reaction times of 0.5 h, 6 h, and 8 h are shown in Fig. 5 a–c, which show that at an initial time of 0.5 h (Fig. 5a), solid microspheres with smooth surfaces and regular shapes were formed. As the reaction time was extended (Fig. 5b), the microsphere surfaces were no longer smooth, and the presence of the small and large particles deposited on the microsphere surfaces was observed significantly. When the reaction time was extended to 8 h (Fig. 5c), cavity structures gradually appeared inside the microspheres, allowing the Bi2MoO6 with hollow structures to be obtained.

Most reports of Bi2MoO6 crystals show 2D nanosheet structures, but in this report, Bi2MoO6 with hollow microsphere structures were prepared to highlight their uniqueness [33]. Fig. 7 a displays Bi2MoO6 which showed primarily spherical aggregates. The fragmented part (Fig. 7b) of the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system was then analyzed to confirm the presence of spherical particles with a size of about 3.17 nm on the surface of Bi2MoO6. The TEM image of CQDs (Fig. 7c) revealed that the particles were almost spherical, and the measurements of 100 particles were averaged to calculate the particle size, which was about 3.28 nm (Fig. 7c internal diagram). The CQDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups and are easily attached to the surface of Bi2MoO6 in a stable manner. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the peak at 22.3° in the XRD pattern corresponds to the CQD particles, and the characteristic diffraction peak of CQDs in the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system was observed (Fig. 8b). The diffraction peaks from CQDs and Bi2MoO6 in the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system were weaker than in the pure phase. Certainly, the CQDs acted as the “external source substance,” which are well-diffused on surface, resulting in a phenomenon where the diffraction peaks of the CQDs are diminished or even fully disappear in the mixture. When the CQDs are doped, the compounding of matter “masks” some of the particles on Bi2MoO6 that can satisfy the diffraction conditions, resulting in a phenomenon where the diffraction peaks of the Bi2MoO6 reduce after combining. This confirms the hypothesis that the CQDs were well diffused on the surface of Bi2MoO6 [20] and agrees with the results of TEM analysis.

Raman analysis was used to characterize the crystal structure of the samples. The band at 802 cm−1 confirms the existence of Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 9 a), and the specific bands at 1390 and 1594 cm−1 confirm the presence of CQDs (Fig. 9b). The intensity of a Raman peak is related to the crystallinity and purity of samples. The combination of CQDs and Bi2MoO6 affected the crystallinity and purity of the samples, which resulted in the weak signals in the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system (Fig. 9c). This conforms the existence of charge transfer process in the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system [2]. Vibration peaks of CQDs in CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system were observed near 1376 cm−1 and 1601 cm−1, which suggests that the CQDs maintain their original structure, even after being bound to the surface of Bi2MoO6 [34].

To obtain energy band information of the Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Bi2MoO6, ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflection spectra was analyzed. After being modified with CQDs, the absorption value was greater than 550 nm (Fig. 10 a). The relationship between α and the photon energy, hv, can be expressed as: (αhν)1/2 = A (hv-Eg)
n/2 . The curve of (αhν)
1/2
-hv can be obtained by directly converting the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra. The band gaps of the Bi2MoO6 and CQDs/Bi2MoO6 were estimated to be 2.02 eV and 1.96 eV, respectively (Fig. 10b). These results indicate that the modification of CQDs enhances the absorption range of visible light by Bi2MoO6.

Visible light irradiation was used to observe the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (Fig. 11 ). In this paper, a comparative photocatalytic experiment was performed under the same conditions. Methylene blue has no self-degradation without the addition of photocatalysts. It is relative stable under visible light for a long time. Furthermore, CQDs have almost no degradation of methylene blue during visible light radiation. Incident light on the surface and internal cavities of Bi2MoO6 can be easily reflected multiple times in the interlayer voids and internal cavities due to the hollow microsphere structure, which helps Bi2MoO6 fully absorb incident photons [35,36]. Notably, the visible light-driven CQDs/Bi2MoO6 showed a higher photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The visible light-response mechanism of CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system
ce:section.ce:para: Electrons and holes generated by photoexcitation can undergo two important competitive processes: separation and recombination. In photocatalytic reactions, photogenerated electrons and holes are separated, and are effectively acted upon by donors and acceptors. However, the separated electrons and holes can recombine inside or on the surface of the materials, which causes luminescence [37]. The effects of CQDs on the separation and recombination of electron-hole pairs from Bi2MoO6 were investigated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photoluminescence spectra, respectively. The radius size of the arc on the EIS Nynquist plot reflects the rate of photogenerated charge separation. As shown in Fig. 12 a, variations in the radius of the arc-radiation EIS Nyquist curve of the ITO/Bi2MoO6/CQDs film and the ITO/Bi2MoO6 film demonstrated an effective increase in the rate at which electron-hole pairs separated inside the Bi2MoO6 [38]. An effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs corresponds to a relatively slow recombination, accompanied by a decrease in the intensity of photoluminescence. As shown in Fig. 12b, Bi2MoO6 in the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system had a lower photoluminescence intensity than that in the pure phase, which showed that a slow recombination of e−/h+ occurred inside the modified Bi2MoO6 [37].

In the methylene blue solution, the electrons produced by Bi2MoO6 under visible light irradiation could react with adsorbed oxygen to produce a strong oxidant of hydroxyl radical, which are active species in the degradation of methylene blue [39], whose specific reaction steps are shown in Fig. 14 . To determine the role of hydroxyl radicals in this study, tert-butanol and H2O2, which are inhibitors and enhancers of hydroxyl radicals, respectively, were added into the solution (Fig. 13a and b). Hydroxyl radicals can preferentially react with tert-butanol due to a faster reaction rate, which then inhibits the entire radical chain reaction [40]. As shown in Fig. 13a, the photocatalytic degradation rate of methylene blue decreased upon continuous addition of tert-butanol, showing an obvious degree of inhibition. In contrast, H2O2 could capture and react with electrons produced by Bi2MoO6 to generate hydroxyl radicals [41], which not only improved the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs but also helped provide abundant hydroxyl radicals. Thus, the rate of the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue increased upon appropriate addition of H2O2 (Fig. 13b). These results indicate that hydroxyl radicals are the main active species in the degradation of methylene blue. K2S2O8, which acts as an electronic quencher, was added into the solution to determine the dominant effect of electrons in this study. Electrons could preferentially react with S2O8 2− which quenched electrons [37]. As shown in Fig. 13c, the photocatalytic degradation rate of methylene blue showed an obvious decrease upon addition of K2S2O8, which indicates that electrons played an important role in the degradation of methylene blue.

In summary, CQDs/Bi2MoO6 demonstrated a superior photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation. The most likely working mechanism is that the modification of CQDs promoted the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs inside the Bi2MoO6 and limited their recombination. According to the experiments performed in this study, CQDs with delocalized conjugated structures are rich in typical “electron-withdrawing groups” such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups [42], which generate a positive electric field on the exterior [43]. Their special electron-trapping ability allows them to easily capture electrons [38] from the conduction band of Bi2MoO6 when irradiated with visible light, which allows electrons to quickly migrate from the inner region to the surface of Bi2MoO6. The electrons that migrated to the surface were able to react with adsorbed oxygen, which allowed them to play an important role in the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue. Then, a series of reactions occurred which ultimately produced an active hydroxyl radical species that effectively degraded methylene blue [44,45]. Thus, the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system was more effective for the degradation of methylene blue when irradiated using visible light. Based on the above discussion, the schematic of the working mechanism is shown in Fig. 14.

3D Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres were successfully prepared using a mixed solvothermal method without the need for added surfactants or other hard template conditions. The formation process of Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres was systematically investigated and discussed. A simple hydrothermal method using waste larch wood powder as the carbon source was used to prepare wood powder-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs contained delocalized conjugated structures and were used as an activator in the CQDs/Bi2MoO6 system to facilitate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which helps electrons quickly transfer from the inner regions to the surface of the modified Bi2MoO6 hollow microspheres. The results obtained in this study indicate that the photocatalytic performance of the Bi2MoO6 to degrade methylene blue under visible light irradiation was enhanced by the modification of the CQDs.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2018YFD0600302) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572018AB01).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tailoring surface groups of carbon quantum dots to improve photoluminescence behaviours
